text: Upon entry into the abdomen, multiple dense adhesions are visible. The left lobe of the liver is densely adhered to the underlying bowel. The surgeon carefully dissects these adhesions to reach the esophagogegenostomy site.

The surgeon uses a Nathanson liver retractor to elevate the liver, revealing the roux limb adhered to the left lateral segment of the liver. The roux limb is carefully dissected free, allowing entry into the mediastinum. The dissection proceeds from medial to lateral using a combination of blunt and sharp techniques.

The surgeon creates a posterior window and passes an umbilical tape for caudad retraction of the esophagus. An anterior plane is identified and dissected down to reveal the right crus, followed by division of dense posterior adhesions.
The surgeon performs circumferential dissection of the mediastinal esophagus, which is visibly thickened and dilated, extending distally to the strictured anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies the roux limb and divides the mesentery approximately five centimeters downstream from the esophageal jejunostomy. The roux limb is then transected using a GIA-Sixty blue load stapler.

Retraction stitches are placed at three and nine o'clock positions on the dilated, thickened esophagus. The esophagus is then transected using surgical shears. The jejunum is brought up, appearing tension-free, and an end-to-side esophago-jejunostomy is performed on the anti-mesenteric side.
The surgeon performs the anastomosis in a single layer using 2-0 Stratafix sutures, beginning at the posterior aspect and progressing to the anterior aspect of the esophago-jejunostomy site.
